coredata.dc:title: Microencapsulated phase change materials with graphene-based materials: Fabrication, characterisation and prospects
coredata.dc:creator: Su, Weiguang
Hu, Meiyong
Wang, Li
Kokogiannakis, Georgios
Chen, Jun
Gao, Liying
Li, Anqing
Xu, Chonghai
coredata.dc:description: Microencapsulated phase change material (MEPCM) is an efficient thermal energy storage material. However, the heat charging/discharging rate of MEPCMs is limited by their low thermal conductivity. Graphene-based materials (i.e. graphene, graphene oxide (GO), reduced graphene oxide (rGO)) have ultrahigh thermal conductivity and have been used as thermal conductive enhancement materials in MEPCMs. This paper reviewed the preparation and characterisation methods of graphene-based materials for MEPCMs. The in-situ polymerization method is the most widely adopted for the preparation of graphene-based microcapsules, and GO-enhanced MEPCM could generate the best morphology result. By embedding graphene in organic shell hybrid structure, the thermal conductivity of microcapsules was increased to as high as 7.2 W/(m∙K). The addition of graphene significantly reduced the supercooling and more than 90% of the leakage rate of MEPCM. It also effectively improved the mechanical strength and photothermal conversion efficiency of MEPCM. In addition, GO exhibits amphiphilicity and can be used as an emulsifier for the preparation of Pickering emulsions. Its amphiphilic properties can be adjusted by: mixing GO with other emulsifiers, altering its pH value, surface modification, and fabricating Janus GO. Graphene can also be used in the preparation of Pickering emulsions after surface modification. Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) is used as a zero-dimensional surfactant due to their oxygen-containing functional groups, which exhibit good amphiphilic properties. In summary, it is beneficial to modify graphene-based materials as surfactants to replace conventional surfactants during the preparation of oil-water emulsions and high-quality MEPCMs.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Microencapsulated phase change material (MEPCM) is an efficient thermal energy storage material. However, the heat charging/discharging rate of MEPCMs is limited by their low thermal conductivity. Graphene-based materials (i.e. graphene, graphene oxide (GO), reduced graphene oxide (rGO)) have ultrahigh thermal conductivity and have been used as thermal conductive enhancement materials in MEPCMs. This paper reviewed the preparation and characterisation methods of graphene-based materials for MEPCMs. The in-situ polymerization method is the most widely adopted for the preparation of graphene-based microcapsules, and GO-enhanced MEPCM could generate the best morphology result. By embedding graphene in organic shell hybrid structure, the thermal conductivity of microcapsules was increased to as high as 7.2 W/(m∙K). The addition of graphene significantly reduced the supercooling and more than 90% of the leakage rate of MEPCM. It also effectively improved the mechanical strength and photothermal conversion efficiency of MEPCM. In addition, GO exhibits amphiphilicity and can be used as an emulsifier for the preparation of Pickering emulsions. Its amphiphilic properties can be adjusted by: mixing GO with other emulsifiers, altering its pH value, surface modification, and fabricating Janus GO. Graphene can also be used in the preparation of Pickering emulsions after surface modification. Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) is used as a zero-dimensional surfactant due to their oxygen-containing functional groups, which exhibit good amphiphilic properties. In summary, it is beneficial to modify graphene-based materials as surfactants to replace conventional surfactants during the preparation of oil-water emulsions and high-quality MEPCMs.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:def-description.ce:para: increased initial weight loss starting temperature

ce:section.ce:para: Microencapsulated phase change materials (MEPCMs) can store thermal energy in an efficient manner. They are fabricated by encapsulating the phase change material (PCM) core into various shells to prevent leakage problems during the melting and solidification process [1,2]. Meanwhile, MEPCM capsules could increase the heat transfer area, thermal stability, and thermal conductivity, minimize the external influence of the environment and reduce the volume change of PCM during the phase change period [3]. MEPCMs as thermal energy storage materials can be used for solar energy collection [4], waste thermal recovery [5] and mini-channel heatsink [6,7], and therefore demonstrates a significant reduction potential of CO2 emissions. Previous research showed that MEPCMs could easily form composite materials with other materials, such as construction materials [8] and textiles [9]. In addition, MEPCM can also suspend in water for thermal energy storage [10] and form a composite with alumina nanoparticles in water as heat transfer ﬂuid [11].

However, the major disadvantage of MEPCMs is their low thermal conductivity (usually 0.15–0.4 W/(m∙K)), which reduces the latent heat charging/discharging rate during phase change [12]. Previous research pointed out that adding high thermal conductivity nanomaterials (i.e. nano-graphite [13], expanded graphite [14], graphene, graphene oxide (GO) and reduced GO (rGO) [15]) into MEPCM can significantly increase the thermal conductivity. The thermal conductivity of monolayer graphene is as high as 4840–5300 W/(m·K) and, hitherto, higher than the thermal conductivity of any other known materials [16,17]. Therefore, graphene-based materials (i.e. graphene, GO, rGO) were trialled for the preparation of MEPCM in recent years. For instance, Zou et al. [18] studied the thermal conductivity enhancement when adding graphene, carbon nanotubes (CNT), and silver nanoparticles for microcapsules into MEPCMs, and found that just a 0.02 w.t.% graphene addition into MEPCMs would achieve the fastest thermal response rate; a rate that was even higher than when adding 0.03 w.t.% CNT and silver nanoparticles. Deng et al. [19] encapsulated paraffin in silicone rubber composed graphene shell by electrostatic self-assembly method, and the thermal conductivity increased by more than three times compared with the silicone rubber shell. Jia et al. [20] prepared MEPCM particles with the microfluidic method and then composed the fabricated MEPCM with various concentrations of GO nanoplatelets in suspension. It was found that the thermal conductivity of the MEPCM/GO suspension reached 0.81 W/(m∙K) when 0.1 w.t.% GO was added, and that this was much greater than the conductivity of the slurry without GO at 0.55 W/(m∙K). Besides enhancing the thermal conductivity of MEPCM, the addition of graphene materials can also improve leakage issues [21], mechanical strength [22], and photothermal conversion performance [23] of MEPCM capsules.

The preparation of oil-water emulsion is a key step for the fabrication of MEPCM with chemical or physical-chemical methods. The stabilization of oil-water emulsions by some high thermal conductivity nanoparticles (also known as Pickering emulsion [24]) has been applied to the preparation of microcapsules, such as nano silicon dioxide (SiO2) hydrosol [25], γ-methacryloxypropyltrimethoxysilane (KH-570) modified SiO2 [26], KH-570 modified titanium dioxide (TiO2) [27]. Graphene-based materials as two-dimensional nanomaterials are also used to prepare Pickering emulsions and MEPCM [28]. In particular, GO can be used as an amphiphilic surfactant for the preparation of water-in-oil (W/O) or oil-in-water (O/W) emulsions during the MEPCM fabrication procedure [29], and it can enhance the thermal conductivity, mechanical strength, thermal stability, tightness, and anti-permeability of MEPCM [30]. The amphiphilicity of GO can be adjusted by surface modifications or changing the pH value [31], and in this way GO can more flexibly satisfy the requirements of MEPCM preparation compared with the other surfactants.

To this end, this paper reviewed and summarized a range of preparation methods of MEPCMs with graphene-based materials. At the same time, the effects of graphene materials on the performance of MEPCM are evaluated, with key performance metrics being thermal conductivity, phase transition temperature, particle size distribution, anti-leakage performance, mechanical properties, and photothermal conversion characteristics. Furthermore, one of the most important applications of graphene-based materials is as surfactants for the preparation of oil-water Pickering emulsion. In this case, the stability of the oil-water emulsion is the key factor for the preparation of MEPCM. Therefore, the modification methods of various graphene-based materials and the applications on Pickering emulsion are also reviewed and compared with a particular focus on the improvement of GO's amphiphilic property. The findings of this paper can be used to extend the application of graphene-based materials as surfactant materials to prepare high-quality MEPCMs and integrate the graphene-based materials into MEPCM as a thermal conductivity enhancement material. The outcomes of this review are expected to provide recommendations for new surfactants and extend the application of graphene, GO, and rGO for the development of MEPCMs in the future.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication methods of MEPCMs with graphene-based materials

ce:section.ce:para: Graphene can be added into MEPCM through various preparation methods, such as in-situ polymerization [32], self-assembly [33], interfacial hydrolysis polycondensation [34], etc.

In-situ polymerization is one of the most common methods for preparing microcapsules, and it is also a common method for adding graphene into microcapsules. Graphene can be incorporated into the shell raw materials when preparing the prepolymer, and the hybrid of graphene and shell can be realized through the polymerization of shell monomers, or by introducing graphene into MEPCM preparation as a surfactant. Graphene can also be mixed with PCM cores for further encapsulation. For example, Su et al. [35] mixed graphene with the prepolymer of methanol modified melamine-formaldehyde (MMF) to form a composed prepolymer solution containing graphene, as shown in Fig. 1 . At the same time, the stable O/W emulsion was prepared by the emulsifier styrene-maleic anhydride (SMA). The prepared prepolymer solution with graphene was added to the O/W emulsion and successfully embedded graphene into the MMF organic hybrid shell by the in-situ polymerization method. Zhao et al. [28] prepared the surface-modified graphene with poly (sodium-p-styrene sulfonate) (PSS) as an amphiphilic surfactant to stabilize the Pickering emulsion, and then successfully introduced graphene into MEPCM capsules. Qiu et al. [36] successfully encapsulated the mixed core of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) and paraffin in poly(melamine-urea-formaldehyde) (PMUF) shell. However, graphene is hydrophobic due to the monolayer two-dimensional honeycomb lattice structure formed by the accumulation of carbon atoms connected by sp2 hybridization. Therefore, the surface modifications of graphene were necessary to prepare the Pickering emulsion for MEPCM fabrication, which means the graphene would typically have to be modified by grafting hydrophilic chains to regulate its amphiphilicity [37].

Moreover, graphene-coated MEPCM capsules can be fabricated through graphene self-assembly on the external surface of the microcapsules. As shown in Fig. 2 , Qiao et al. [38] prepared the MEPCM within PMUF polymer shell, and then dispersed the MEPCM capsules into sodium dodecyl benzene sulphonate (SDBS) solution with anionic charges, while the graphene suspension was mixed with cationic surfactant cetyltrimethylammonium bromide (CTAB). Different charges of anions and cations would attract each other and absorb graphene on the surface of MEPCM. On the other hand, Dao et al. [39] verified that graphene can be used as a shell to fabricate MEPCM by self-assembly. During the MEPCM preparation, the melted stearic acid (SA) was dispersed in the water phase as a core with an anionic surfactant of sodium dodecyl sulfate (SDS), and then the emulsion was mixed with an aqueous dispersion of graphene with a cationic surfactant of CTAB. In the end, the self-assembled graphene on the surface of SA particles forms core-shell MEPCM due to the electrostatic interaction. This method is more convenient than other methods, but the morphology of MEPCM was rough, non-uniform, and easily deformed during phase change cycles due to the poor mechanical properties of the graphene shell.

Interface hydrolysis polycondensation can also be used to fabricate MEPCM with graphene. As shown in Fig. 3 , Zhu et al. [40] prepared nano encapsulated phase change materials (NEPCM) with SiO2/graphene composite shells. The oil phase (including tetraethyl orthosilicate (TEOS), methyl trimethoxy silane (MTMS) and n-octadecane) was stabilized by the emulsifier of CTAB as an O/W emulsion. In this case, the PCM droplets had a positive charged surface and the graphene was dispersed into the water phase by SDS with negative charges. When graphene dispersion drops into the O/W emulsion, graphene self-assembles on the surface of the O/W emulsion droplet due to the force of electrostatic interaction. After the addition of ammonia, the SiO2 shell produced by the interfacial hydrolysis polycondensation of TEOS and MTMS, resulted in the encapsulation of the PCM core to fabricate the SiO2/graphene NEPCM.

Moreover, interfacial polymerization and emulsion polymerization methods are also used to prepare MEPCM with graphene. Tahan et al. [41] successfully added graphene nanoplates (GNP) into PCM cores which were then encapsulated by isophorone-di-isocyanate (IPDI) and diethylenetriamine (DETA) shell through interfacial polymerization. Wu et al. [42] dispersed graphene in SDS and polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) aqueous solution to obtain stable graphene suspension by ultrasound and produced the graphene-doped MEPCM by emulsion polymerization. The SDS as a surfactant reduced the surface tension of the aqueous solution and promoted the dispersion of graphene, and the PVA as a stabilizer inhibits the aggregation of graphene in solution.

Table 1 summarizes various methods and scanning electron microscope (SEM) images of MEPCM that was prepared with graphene. It can be found that the addition position of graphene and preparation methods do have a great influence on the surface roughness and morphology of MEPCMs. The morphology of MEPCM prepared by in-situ polymerization is better than other methods, as the SEM images of Liu et al. [13] and Qiu et al. [36] show in Table 1. However, even for the same method, the morphology of MEPCMs will differ due to the different adding positions of graphene. For example, when graphene is inside the MEPCMs the capsule surface is smooth with better sphericity. However, there will be some ‘burrs’ and roughness on the surface when the graphene is added to the surface of the MEPCMs or embedded in the shell. The MEPCM prepared by the self-assembly method has the largest difference in morphology, and cannot even maintain good sphericity when graphene is used as the shell. This is mainly due to the irregular and non-uniform self-assembly of graphene on the surface of core microspheres. MEPCMs prepared by emulsion polymerization are prone to obvious small pits, which are due to the larger internal space of MEPCMs after solidification of the PCM core [43].

For the preparation of MEPCM with GO, in-situ polymerization is still one of the most widely used methods. At the same time, hydrolytic polycondensation [51], Pickering emulsion polymerization [52], self-assembly [53], and interfacial polymerization [21] methods are also commonly used in the preparation of MEPCM with GO. Moreover, compared with graphene, GO is more widely used in microcapsules because it has the advantages of low production cost, large-scale production, easy processing, and the groups on the surface of GO are more conducive to the stability of oil-water emulsions [54].

Oxygen-containing chemical groups (such as hydroxyl group) on the GO surface result in amphiphilic properties and therefore, GO or GO mixed with other emulsifiers can be used to stabilize the O/W emulsion for the preparation of MEPCM. For this reason, the Pickering emulsion method is one of the most common methods for the preparation of microcapsules with GO. For instance, Wei et al. [55] used SMA combined with GO to emulsify paraffin to prepare a Pickering emulsion and realize multifunctional MEPCM with GO and lead tungstate (PbWO4) as double shells, as shown in Fig. 4 . Maithya et al. [56] used GO as a colloid stabilizer to emulsify the mixture of docosane and water to obtain a stable Pickering emulsion, and successfully prepared GO-modified polyurea (PUA) MEPCM by suspension polymerization. Zhang et al. [52] modified GO with [2-(methacryloxy) ethyl] trimethylammonium chloride (METAC) to stabilize O/W Pickering emulsion and then fabricated polystyrene (PS)/GO composite shell for MEPCM by Pickering emulsion template method. Maithya et al. [21] used recycled chitin (RCh) as an emulsifier for Pickering emulsion to realize the interfacial polycondensation of IPDI and ethylene diamine, and added GO suspension during the process of polycondensation to realize the hybridization between GO and shell.

Hydrolysis polycondensation is another method that can be used for the preparation of MEPCM with GO. It is usually adopted when SiO2 or TiO2 are used as the shell and are combined with the self-assembly of GO to realize the introduction of GO into MEPCM. For example, Ji et al. [51] achieved TiO2 encapsulated PCM by hydrolysis and polycondensation of titanium butoxide (TBT) and then added GO to the surface of MEPCM by self-assembly, as shown in Fig. 5 . Yuan et al. [57] also fabricated SiO2/GO MEPCM by GO self-assembly.

In addition, GO-coated MEPCM could convert the GO to graphene with the addition of reductants. For instance, Zhou et al. [53] used GO and GNP as shell materials and paraffin as core materials to fabricate MEPCM. The octadecyl trimethyl ammonium bromide (OTAB) was used to adjust the hydrophilicity and lipophilicity of GO to obtain a uniform and stable Pickering emulsion. The hybrid shell was constructed by π-π interaction between GO and GNP. Finally, the GO/GNP MEPCM was obtained by freezing drying. In the end, GO was reduced by ascorbic acid (VC) to achieve various ratios rGO/GNP encapsulated MEPCM. Yang et al. [58] wrapped paraffin with GO to get paraffin@GO MEPCM, then reduced GO with hydrazine hydrate to get paraffin@graphene MEPCM, see Fig. 6 .

Table 2 shows the methods and morphologies of MEPCM prepared with GO. Compared with the MEPCM prepared with graphene, the MEPCM prepared by GO has better morphology and smooth surface. That is due to the presence of hydrophilic groups on the surface of GO, which enables GO to be more evenly dispersed and to stabilize the oil-water emulsion during the preparation of MEPCM [59]. However, when the individual GO or GNP was used as a shell to encapsulate PCM, the morphology of MEPCM is also irregular due to the problem of non-uniform accumulation of surface GO or GNP during the self-assembly process. In addition, in-situ polymerization could maintain a good morphology of microcapsules in MEPCM prepared by GO but also increased the surface roughness of microcapsules. Hydrolysis polycondensation method is another commonly used method for the preparation of MEPCM by GO. This method is usually combined with the self-assembly method to realize the addition of GO, which also leads to some surface roughness. GO Pickering emulsion is an alternative method for the preparation of MEPCM. The amphiphilicity of GO makes it easily attached to the surface of the oil phase that is placed between the PCM core and the solid shell of microcapsules.

Thermal conductivity strongly affects the heat storage/release rate of MEPCM during phase change and is a key parameter for various applications. Thermal conductivity values of MEPCM can be obtained with several standard measurement instruments, such as laser thermal diffusivity conductivity instrument [71], thermal property analyser [72], and thermal conductivity apparatus [73]. The thermal conductivity improvement of MEPCM can be affected by the properties of graphene, GO and rGO materials, the addition location, and some fabrication parameters, i.e. pH value during microencapsulation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Thermal conductivity enhancement by the properties of graphene, GO and rGO
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 7 shows the thermal conductivity enhancement by the various dosages of graphene, GO, and rGO for MEPCM capsules. The statistical results show that the graphene material leads to the highest improvement of the thermal conductivity of microcapsules with maximum thermal conductivity measured as 7.2 W/(m∙K), while the maximum thermal conductivity for rGO and GO was 0.8 W/(m∙K) and 1.0 W/(m∙K) respectively. Liu et al. [74] prepared three types of MEPCM with the GO at different oxidation degrees which were produced by oxidized graphene for 30, 60, and 90 min. It was found that the shorter the oxidation time of graphene, the higher the thermal conductivity of microcapsules.

To increase the thermal conductivity of microcapsules, GO can be reduced to graphene using reductive materials after the GO was added into microcapsules. For example, Yang et al. [58] reduced GO@MEPCM to graphene@MEPCM with hydrazine hydrate. The thermal conductivity increased from 0.182 W/(m∙K) to 0.351 W/(m∙K). Zhou et al. [53] prepared MEPCM with GO on paraffin surface by self-assembly method, and constructed hybrid shell by π-π interaction between GO and GNP. Finally, GO was reduced by VC to prepare MEPCM with different proportions of rGO/GNP encapsulated paraffin. The results showed that compared with the rGO shell, the mixed shell could further improve the thermal conductivity of MEPCM from 0.67 W/(m∙K) to 0.90 W/(m∙K). Wang et al. [75] prepared rGO/MEPCM and then increased the thermal conductivity of microcapsules through reduction of GO to graphene by sodium borohydride (NaBH4) and thermal reduction, respectively. The results showed that when 1 w.t.% NaBH4 was added to reduce GO in MEPCM, the thermal conductivity of the MEPCM increased by 83%. On the other hand, the thermal conductivity of MEPCM was increased by as much as 193% by thermal reduction.

Using GO or GNP as a shell to prepare MEPCM could also lead to a significant increase of thermal conductivity. Guo et al. [33] prepared MEPCM that was coated only with graphene by electrostatic interaction self-assembly method, and the thermal conductivity increased from 0.28 W/(m∙K) to 1.73 W/(m∙K). Dao et al. [39] also prepared SA@graphene MEPCMs that encapsulated SA with graphene as the shell. In terms of comparing directly mixed SA with graphene, encapsulated PCM in the graphene shell can more effectively improve the thermal conductivity. Liu et al. [63] studied the thermal conductivity enhancement of MEPCM by GO, CNT, and the mixture of GO/CNT (GO: CNT = 3:1, 1:1, 1:3). They found that the initial thermal conductivity of MEPCM was only 0.13 W/(m∙K), while this value increased to 0.27 W/(m∙K) and 0.18 W/(m∙K) when GO and CNT was added into MEPCM, respectively. The mixture of GO and CNT showed better thermal conductivity enhancement for MEPCM than by just using separately GO or CNT, with the highest measured thermal conductivity being 0.4 W/(m∙K) when GO:CNT = 3:1.

In addition, it can be seen from Fig. 7 that some MEPCMs without graphene materials have also high thermal conductivity. This is related to the thermal conductivity of the different shell materials. For example, Zhu et al. [40] prepared MEPCM with SiO2 as the shell by TEOS hydrolysis polycondensation, and the thermal conductivity reached 0.642 W/(m∙K) without graphene.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Thermal conductivity enhancement by the addition location of graphene, GO and rGO
ce:section.ce:para: For the same type and dosage of graphene-based materials, the nanomaterial addition location, such as on the MEPCM surface, embedded in the shell, or mixed with the PCM core material, would also affect the thermal conductivity of MEPCM. Qiao et al. [38] used paraffin as PCM and encapsulate PCM into PMUF shell, and then added graphene material on the MEPCM surface to form graphene-coated MEPCM. The thermal conductivity increased from 0.194 W/(m∙K) to 1.054 W/(m∙K) after adding 10 w.t.% graphene. Wang et al. [47] embedded 3 w.t.% graphene into the MMF polymer organic shell to hybridize it, and the thermal conductivity increased from 0.2 W/(m∙K) to 7.08 W/(m∙K). Chen et al. [60] mixed 0.5 w.t.% GO with the n-octadecane core, and the thermal conductivity of MEPCM increased from 0.18 W/(m∙K) to 0.26 W/(m∙K). Qiao et al. [77] compared the addition of GO on the surface of microcapsules (PCM@UF/GO) as opposed to mixing GO with paraffin as the core material (PCM/GO@UF). It was found that the thermal conductivity of MEPCM was only 0.252 W/(m∙K) when 10 w.t.% GO was mixed with the core material, but the value reached 1.067 W/(m∙K) when GO was added on the surface of microcapsules. That is because when GO is added to the core material, GO cannot form a continuous highly conductive heat transfer path between the PCM core and the external environment and as a result the thermal conductivity of MEPCM cannot be substantially increased.

Fig. 8 shows the effect of the addition location of graphene/GO materials on the thermal conductivity of MEPCM based on the reviewed studies. It can be seen that the best performance in terms of thermal conductivity enhancement of MEPCM can be obtained by embedding graphene/GO materials into the shell hybrid (i.e. in Fig. 8: Graphene hybrid, GO hybrid), followed by embedding these materials on the surface of MEPCM (i.e. Graphene surface, GO surface). The lowest thermal conductivity values are observed when GO is placed within the core material (GO inside in Fig. 8). Embedding graphene/GO material into the shells can form a continuous thermal conductive matrix between the external MEPCM shells and the core. The core material is well connected with the external environment through graphene/GO material, which significantly increases the thermal conductivity of MEPCM. Fig. 8 also shows that the thermal conductivity increases gradually with the mass increase of graphene/GO when the graphene/GO content is less than 2 w.t.%. The thermal conductivity is especially high (7.2 W/(m∙K)) for the Graphene hybrid MEPCM when 2 w.t.% graphene content was added. However, after the graphene is hybridized with the organic shell and the content is more than 2 w.t.%, the thermal conductivity of the MEPCM does not always increase, and in some cases it even decreases. That is because the increase of graphene content in the shell cannot exceed the electrostatic adhesion ability of emulsion droplets during the in-situ polymerization process. The content of graphene in the shell tends to be saturated when the graphene increases to 2 w.t.%. Theoretically, graphene materials can be added to the surface of MEPCM without saturation, but the thermal conductivity improvement rate is even less than half of that when embedded in the shell, and it can be easily separated from the surface of the MEPCM. For example, Khezri et al. [45] added graphene nanosheets to the surface of microcapsules and found that the thermal conductivity increased only from 0.29 W/(m∙K) to 0.43 W/(m∙K) with 10 w.t.% graphene content. Compared with GO, graphene was easier to mix with the organic PCM cores, but the thermal conductivity was only 0.3–0.4 W/(m∙K) when graphene or GO was added into the MEPCM core. That is because the decrease of the internal thermal resistance of MECPM only has very little influence on the internal and external heat transfer. However, the addition of graphene/GO would reduce the weight percentage of PCM and result in lower latent heat value. It is therefore necessary to optimize the weight ratios between thermal conductive enhancement materials and core material.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Thermal conductivity enhancement in relation to pH value during MEPCM fabrication
ce:section.ce:para: Graphene with a monolayer carbon atom structure is difficult to react with organic materials and deposit into the microcapsule shell. Generally, graphene is deposited on the MEPCM shell by the electrostatic interaction and molecular entanglement effects. Therefore, the pH value has a significant influence on the deposition of graphene [22]. The deposition of graphene on the MEPCM shell reaches the maximum at a particular pH value. Guo et al. [48] prepared MEPCM with paraffin core and graphene/organic hybrid shell by self-assembly polymerization. Five microcapsules were prepared at pH values of 3–7, and the maximum thermal conductivity reached 6.681 W/(m∙K) at pH 5. When the pH value was increased from 3 to 5, the thermal conductivity increased from 2 W/(m∙K) to 6.681 W/(m∙K), but when continued to increase the pH value to 7, the thermal conductivity decreased to 5 W/(m∙K). This indicates that the content of graphene on the shell of microcapsules was affected by the pH value and shows the importance of optimizing it in the MEPCM preparation process.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Phase change temperature and latent heat
ce:section.ce:para: The latent heat is one of the most important properties of MEPCM, which is usually measured by differential scanning calorimetry (DSC). Meanwhile, the encapsulation rate of MEPCM can also be calculated by the ratio of the phase change enthalpy (ΔH) of MEPCM against the phase change enthalpy of the PCM core [78].

It can be seen from Fig. 9 that the addition of graphene or GO would slightly reduce the latent heat value of MEPCM samples. This is because the addition of graphene materials is equivalent to the increase of shell content and reduces the weight percentage of PCM core material [28,42]. Fig. 9 shows that the higher the encapsulation rate of MEPCM, the greater the latent heat value, which is affected by the core ratio and the PCM latent thermal energy storage capacity. Compared with MEPCM without GO and graphene (core content is 50–95 w.t.%), graphene is mostly used in microcapsules with core content of 50–75 w.t.%, while GO has a wider range of core-shell ratios and can be used in microcapsules with core content of 35–95 w.t.%.

However, graphene or GO materials used as a shell to encapsulate PCM can even increase the latent heat and the encapsulation rate can be more than 95%. Yang et al. [58] encapsulated a PCM with GO as the shell and then reduced the GO to graphene by hydrazine hydrate. The experimental measured results showed the latent heat thermal energy storage capacity of the graphene@MEPCM (232.8 J/g) was slightly higher than that of pure PCM (227.6 J/g). That means the encapsulation rate of MEPCM was higher than 100% according to the definition of the encapsulation rate. This is because graphene promotes the crystallization of alkanes and improves the crystallinity of alkanes during the solidification process. Guo et al. [33] also used graphene as the shell to encapsulate paraffin and found that the graphene@MEPCM's latent heat value was 256.9 J/g when 0.5 w.t.% graphene was added, while the pure PCM latent heat value was 251.4 J/g. However, when 8 w.t.% graphene was added as the shell, the latent heat value of graphene@MEPCM decreased to 230.3 J/g. This is because the van der Waals force between paraffin molecules is weaker than that between the graphene and paraffin molecules. The increase of intermolecular van der Waals force leads to a greater need of latent heat of fusion during the phase change process.

The addition of graphene could significantly improve the supercooling behaviour of PCM in microcapsules because graphene can be used as a nucleating agent [80,81]. The capsule size and the nucleating agent are the two main factors affecting the supercooling of MEPCM. Zhu et al. [40] prepared NEPCM with SiO2/graphene shell by the interfacial hydrolysis and polycondensation of TEOS and the self-assembly of graphene in emulsion. During the DSC measurement, the n-octadecane exhibited two peaks at 24.2 °C and 23.0 °C in the crystallization. However, the NEPCM samples exhibited three peaks at 4.85 °C, 14.98 °C, and 22.29 °C in the crystallization of SiO2@NEPCM without graphene and demonstrated a serious supercooling problem with a subcooling temperature of 23.46 °C. On the other hand, the peak at 4.85 °C completely disappeared after the addition of graphene and the subcooling temperature became 10.32 °C, a decrease of 13.14 °C, indicating that the addition of graphene effectively reduced the supercooling of NEPCM. Qiu et al. [36] added GQDs to the core material and found the supercooling temperature decreased from 13.50 °C to 4.98 °C after adding GQDs. This was because the high thermal conductivity of GQDs enhances heat transfer, and the particle size of GQDs is small enough to be used as a good nucleating agent.

The thermal stability of microcapsules determines the maximum working temperature and long-term working stability, which is a key indicator of MEPCM. Thermal stability is usually characterized by thermogravimetric analysis (TGA). Fig. 10 shows the thermal stability of MEPCM after adding graphene/GO. Fig. 10 (a) represents the initial weight loss starting temperature (T) when graphene or GO is added according to the experimental results of various studies. The results showed that the T value of MEPCM without graphene/GO was generally in the range of 140–250 °C, and this range was increased to 150–300 °C and 150–260 °C after adding graphene and GO in MEPCM. Meanwhile, the absolute increase of initial weight loss starting temperature (ΔT) is calculated by comparing the T changes before and after adding graphene-based materials in the same study, as shown in Fig. 10 (b). The maximum value of ΔT for MEPCM composed with graphene was as much as 120 °C, but the thermal stability was improved by just 35 °C when GO was added. The addition of graphene can form an inorganic/organic composite shell, which acts as a protective layer, resisting the heat to destroy the polymer shell structure. Moreover, the thermal stability for microcapsules is also affected by different shell materials, resulting in different ΔT after adding graphene materials. For example, Ma et al. [66] used TiO2 as shell material to prepare MEPCM and the initial weight loss start temperature was 250 °C, but the weight loss temperature was reduced to 248.3 °C after adding GO. This showed that the TiO2 as shell material could achieve high stability, and it was even better than the thermal stability after adding GO.

The particle size of MEPCM can be measured by SEM [82] and laser scattering particle size distribution analyser [26]. The particle size of MEPCM is mainly determined by the dispersion speed of O/W emulsion during the MEPCM preparation. Usually, the particle size can be reduced by the increased mechanical dispersion speed [83]. Table 3 shows that particle sizes of 50–100 μm MEPCM are usually fabricated by the mechanical dispersion speed below 2000 rpm, and particle sizes of 5–20 μm and 1–5 μm are produced by the dispersion speed of 2000–10000 rpm and over 10,000 rpm, respectively. On the other hand, the preparation of NEPCM [84] or miniemulsion polymerization is usually carried out by ultrasonic dispersion to reduce the particle size to the nanoscale. Moreover, the viscosity of the O/W emulsion was increased by the volume of the oil phase, and therefore it needs a higher dispersion speed to generate shear force to reduce the particle size of the microcapsules. For example, when Zhang et al. [30] used 40 g bulk paraffin as an oil phase in 210 ml water, the particle size of the O/W emulsion remained at 6–10 μm even with a stirring speed as high as 16,000 rpm.

In addition to the stirring speed, emulsifiers, preparation methods and the filling of graphene-based materials also affect the particle size of MEPCM. Table 3 lists the effects of different emulsifiers and different preparation methods on the particle size when graphene materials are used for MEPCM fabrication. It is found that the particle size distribution of microcapsules can be effectively improved when graphene materials are mixed with core materials. Qiu et al. [36] added GQDs to paraffin as the core material and found the addition of GQDs could make the particle size of microcapsules smaller with a narrower range. This may be because GQDs can suppress the cohesion among core materials. Chen et al. [60] mixed ODA modified GO with octadecane as the core material to prepare microcapsules and found that the particle size also decreased with the increase of GO content. The reason for this decrease was because the GO modified by ODA has a hydrophilic and lipophilic structure, and the alkylated GO as a co-emulsifier stabilizes oil emulsion droplets and reduces the recombination of adjacent oil droplets.

Leakage is one of the important negative factors affecting the energy storage capacities of MEPCM. The core material in MEPCM may be leaking through the pores of the capsule shell after melting if the shell is not good enough. To this end, the leakage rate was used for evaluating the leakage prevention performance of the MEPCM and it was calculated by the mass difference between the initial MEPCM and the MEPCM after the leakage [86].

The addition of self-assembled graphene on the outer surface is equivalent to secondary shells on the surface of microcapsules, and therefore acts as double protection of PCM cores while effectively reducing the leakage problem. For instance, Zhao et al. [28] used modified graphene as the Pickering emulsion stabilizer to prepare microcapsules and the graphene could form a protective layer on the surface of paraffin droplets. It was found that the leakage rate of MEPCM with 0.2 w.t.% graphene decreased by 93.2% compared with that MEPCM capsules without graphene. MF shell was polymerized on the surface of core droplets when graphene stabilized the oil phase, and the prepared MEPCM had graphene and MF double-shell structure, as shown in Fig. 11 (a). Zhang et al. [30] used the amphiphilic GO to emulsify paraffin and then prepared microcapsules with MF as shell material by in-situ polymerization. GO was placed at the interface of core and shell, as demonstrated in Fig. 11 (b). After GO was added, the leakage rate decreased by 93.1%. Without the addition of GO, paraffin will directly leak from the pores or crevices of the MF shell, while the addition of GO can extend the leakage path since the MF shell and GO shelter each other.

During the phase transformation process, the volume shrinkage and expansion of MEPCM capsules might lead to shell rupture if the shell is not strong enough. Therefore, the mechanical properties of MEPCM are very important for their long-term stability [87]. The mechanical properties mainly depend on the shell thickness and microstructure [88], which can be evaluated by atomic force microscope (AFM) or nanoindentation [89].

Nanoindentation is considered to be the most convenient and standard method to evaluate the mechanical properties of single microcapsules [35], see Fig. 12 (a). Usually, the single microcapsule has elastic-plastic deformation and can recover to the original shape if the compressed pressure is unloaded before the yield point. However, when the pressure exceeds the yield point, MEPCM will undergo permanent deformation and irreversible deformation will further expand until the microcapsules rupture. Based on elastic contact theory [22], Young's modulus and hardness of microcapsules can be calculated by nanoindentation. The yield point is a mechanical parameter reflecting the resistance to plastic deformation. Guo et al. [48] tested the mechanical properties of MEPCM within graphene/HMMM organic hybrid structure shells fabricated under different pH by the nanoindentation method. As shown in Fig. 12 (b), the yield point value has an increasing trend with the pH value from 3 to 5, and the maximum value is as much as 1.85 MPa for pH value of 5. Then yield point values decrease to 1.70 MPa and 1.42 MPa for pH values of 6 and 7, respectively. SEM images II and III in Fig. 12 (b) show the failure before and after the yield limit. Graphene addition in shells is therefore a feasible way to enhance the mechanical properties of the microcapsules. By regulating the pH values of polymerization, the mechanical properties can be controlled due to the various contents of graphene in shells. Su et al. [35] also used the nanoindentation technique to analyse MEPCM within graphene/MMF organic hybrid structure shells. The results are shown in Fig. 12 (c), (d), proving that Young's modulus and Yield limit of MEPCM with graphene/organic hybrid structure shell are significantly improved compared with the MEPCM without graphene. With the increase of graphene content from 0% to 6%, Young's modulus increases by 1.5–2.3 GPa, and the Yield limit increases by 0.55–0.8 MPa.

Moreover, Jiang et al. [90] tested the mechanical properties of microcapsules with GO and CaCO3 as shells by centrifugal acceleration test and then calculated the breakage rate by mass changes. It was found that when the GO content was 1.0 w.t.%, the damage rate of microcapsules decreased by 54% compared with MEPCM without GO. This was because GO has a large surface area and good dispersion and compatibility with the CaCO3 shell, which enables GO to be able to share, transfer and absorb part of the external force energy.

MEPCM can be used for collection, storage, and realizing the effective utilization of solar energy. The addition of graphene/GO materials can improve the efficiency of photothermal conversion [23,40]. The photothermal conversion performance of microcapsules is generally obtained through photothermal experiments [67]. As shown in Fig. 13 (a), the prepared MEPCM is pressed into a thermally insulated box, and temperatures irradiated by the simulated light source are recorded by a thermocouple. Maithya et al. [21] prepared MEPCM with GO as the photon captor and found the microcapsule slurry containing GO can effectively improve the photothermal conversion rate. The addition of GO makes the colour of MEPCM slurry change from milky white to grey, and grey slurry shows a better absorption of sunlight than white slurry [69]. Zhao et al. [28] studied the effect of graphene and GO content on the photothermal conversion of MEPCM, and found that graphene was more beneficial than GO for improving the photothermal conversion efficiency of microcapsules. The experimental data in Fig. 13 (b) [28,56,67] show that the photothermal conversion efficiency of MEPCM could reach 90% when the graphene content was 0.1 w.t.%, while the conversion efficiency was approximately 60% when the GO content was 8 w.t.%.

Generally, the production process of graphene is more complex than GO. On the other hand, GO shows excellent amphiphilicity and its use makes it easier to prepare the oil-water emulsion and microcapsules. The effect of graphene and GO addition on the performance of MEPCM is summarized in Table 4 . Graphene materials can effectively improve the thermal conductivity of MEPCM and have better (higher) thermal conductivity than GO. Moreover, the addition of graphene-based materials improved the thermal stability, mechanical properties, and photothermal conversion rate of microcapsules. It also effectively reduced the leakage rate of microcapsules since the addition of graphene materials was equivalent to the secondary shell on the surface of microcapsules. However, graphene did not noticeably affect the latent heat and encapsulation efficiency of microcapsules as these two factors mainly depended on the preparation methods of MEPCM.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Modification methods of graphene-based materials as surfactants
ce:section.ce:para: As reviewed in Section 3, GO and modified graphene as surfactant materials can be used for the preparation of Pickering emulsions to produce various oil-water emulsions. This is also one of the most important fundamentals for realizing the application of graphene-based materials in MEPCM. To this end, this section summarizes the preparation and modification methods of GO, rGO, and graphene nanosheets to guide the development of graphene-based materials as surfactants for MEPCM development.

ce:section.ce:section-title: GO for the fabrication of O/W emulsion

ce:section.ce:para: GO can be obtained by oxidation and exfoliation of graphite, which is usually made into an aqueous solution to generate hydrophilic carbon-based sheets [91]. The surface of GO is decorated with various oxygen-containing functional groups, such as the hydrophilic edges (carboxylic acid groups) and more hydrophobic basal plane (epoxy and hydroxyl groups), providing amphiphilic character [92]. GO is an amphiphilic material as first was proved by Cote et al. in 2009 [93], who found that there are many oxygen-containing groups in GO nanoplates, such as carboxyl groups at the edge of GO sheets, and hydroxyl and epoxy groups in the middle. This means GO can interact with nonpolar and polar substances at the same time. It can be therefore used as an amphiphilic surfactant to realize the dispersion of non-polar materials in the water phase or water droplets in the oil phase. This indicates an interesting property of GO sheets which enables GO to function as surfactants in mixtures of hydrophobic liquids and water [94–96].

Table 5 lists the applications of GO as a surfactant to stabilize oil-water emulsions. Kumar et al. [97] used GO as a surfactant to disperse styrene into the aqueous water phase to form O/W emulsion and formed PS particles coated with GO. Guo et al. [98] used amphiphilic GO as a surfactant in an oil-water system to form W/O emulsion; then the water in the W/O microspheres was evaporated by heating to obtain the GO hollow microspheres as anode materials for lithium-ion batteries. Xie et al. [99] used GO as an individual surfactant to prepare PS particles by Pickering emulsion polymerization. The authors then studied the effects of aqueous initiator and oil initiator and found that the oil initiator could produce a better and more uniform particle morphology. Huang et al. [100] used GO as an emulsifier and prepared PMMA/GO composites by Pickering emulsion polymerization. The average diameter of the nano-composite particles was about 150 nm. As a surfactant, GO can disperse various oil monomers in water and complete polymerization to produce various polymer particles, such as styrene [99], benzyl methacrylate [101], methyl methacrylate (MMA) [100]. At the same time, Zhang et al. [64] used GO in the preparation of MEPCM and confirmed that it is feasible to use GO as a surfactant in the preparation of MEPCM.

The stability for some oil-water emulsions is poor when GO is used as a surfactant alone. It is therefore common to combine GO with other emulsifiers to prepare oil-water emulsions. Zhang et al. [64] compared the particle size distribution of paraffin-in-water emulsion when using GO only and GO mixed with Span-80 and Tween-80. The particle size range of the emulsion was reduced from 3-20 μm to 3–11 μm when the GO composed emulsifiers replaced GO. Zhao et al. [108] used polyethylene glycol 600 (PEG-600)+PVA + SMA as composed emulsifiers to emulsify paraffin and then mixed the emulsion with GO suspension to obtain GO-mixed O/W emulsion. On the other hand, another GO Pickering PCM emulsion was prepared by the composed surfactant of GO + PEG-600+PVA + SMA directly. By comparing the two preparation methods, it was found that the particle size distribution of GO + PEG-600+PVA + SMA was 5.43–9.26 μm, while that of GO-mixed with the fabricated O/W emulsion was 2.84–9.24 μm. The reason for the difference was that the GO-mixed with the fabricated O/W emulsion affected the state of the original paraffin O/W emulsion, resulting in a larger particle size range of the emulsion. In regards to GO Pickering phase change emulsion, GO is acting as Pickering emulsion stabilizer to coat paraffin, which improves the stability of emulsion droplets and the particle size is relatively uniform. Table 6 shows the applications of GO with various emulsifiers, which have become a common technique for the preparation of MEPCMs and PCM emulsions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: GO amphiphilicity adjusted by pH value and ions
ce:section.ce:para: In some cases, the emulsifying ability of nanoparticles for oil-water emulsions is related to the ζ potential. However, a high number of charges on the surface of nanoparticles leads to excessive electrostatic interaction between the dispersed particles and results in a negative influence on emulsion stability. GO shows a considerable high negative charge (zeta potential of about −60 mV) and hydrophilicity, which is not sufficient to stabilize the emulsion droplets [111]. The hydrophilicity of GO mainly depends on the ionization degree of the –COOH group, which means that GO shows more hydrophilicity if the ionization degree is increased [91]. Since the ionization of the –COOH group is related to pH, the amphiphilicity of GO can be adjusted by altering the pH value. Kim et al. [95] found that when the pH value is 10, GO would be deprotonated which would make GO more hydrophilic and fail to obtain O/W emulsions. However, when the pH value is between 2 and 5, GO is protonated and becomes gradually hydrophobic with better hydrophilic-lipophilic balance and can better stabilize oil-water emulsions. Che et al. [106] changed the surface activity of GO by adjusting the pH value. The experimental result showed that the particle size distribution of emulsion at pH = 3.2 was narrower than at pH values of 2.4 and 5.2 because pH could affect the ionization degree of –COOH group and thus changed its hydrophilicity.

On the other hand, the increased ion concentration leads to the shrinkage of the electric double layer and the decrease of the electrostatic repulsion. This increases the hydrophobicity of GO and promotes the migration of GO to the hydrophobic interface. It also makes GO dispersed in water more easily to coagulate [112,113]. Yoon et al. [114] found GO could form a stable dispersion solution at pH between 2 and 10 when 5 w.t.% NaCl was added. GO-stabilized aqueous emulsions were due to the combination of the strong electrostatic repulsion of highly charged GO edges and the high ionic strength, whereby the salt drives the GO sheets to the O/W interface [115]. Zhang et al. [116] found that it was difficult to adsorb GO on the surface of oil particles without adding NaCl. The possible reason was that the electrostatic attraction between negatively charged particles and the positively charged interface was not large enough to overcome the energy barrier between them. However, a stable O/W emulsion was formed by GO when the salt concentration was higher than a certain value (≥0.1 mol/L). He et al. [113] compared the salt with different cation values in benzyl chloride/water emulsions stabilized by GO and they also found that the cation valence in the electrolyte plays an important role in stabilizing the O/W emulsion. The results proved that the divalent electrolyte (MgCl2) gives a more significant stabilizing effect in comparison with a monovalent electrolyte (NaCl).

The surface functional groups of GO are amphiphilic and can graft with the other groups. The most common materials and technologies used for GO modification are analysed in the following paragraphs, and are shown in Table 7 .

Silane coupling agent is a low molecular weight organic silicon compound, which can adsorb or react with inorganic materials after hydrolysis. Its chemical formula is Y–Si-X3, where Y is an organic functional group (i.e. –NH2, –CC, and epoxy group) that can react with polymer, while X is a hydrolyzable group which is usually alkoxy [117,118]. Common silane coupling agents include KH-550, KH560, KH-570, etc. These coupling agents contain a carbon-carbon double bond end and alkoxy on the other end. Table 7 shows the modification parameters of GO with silane coupling agents. In addition, MTMS and (3-Aminopropyl) triethoxysilane (3-APTS) were also used for surface modification of GO and were successfully applied in MEPCM preparation and as a catalyst. The modification process is shown in Fig. 14 . Firstly, Si–O–CH3 of KH-570 is hydrolyzed in acidic ethanol solution to form Si–OH. Then, Si–OH reacts with the hydroxyl group on the surface of GO to form hydrogen bonds and the -Si-O-C covalent bond is formed by condensation reaction. On the other hand, -Si-OH groups react with each other to form a Si–O–Si bonds network on the surface of GO or enter the GO layer to functionalize GO [119]. Zhou et al. [76] used KH-570 to modify the surface of GO and reduce its hydrophilicity. Then, the functional GO is used to disperse the oil phase for microencapsulation. Zhang et al. [118] also used KH-570 modified GO as the surfactant and successfully prepared microcapsules with polystyrene shells by suspension polymerization. After encapsulation, the average particle size was 109.978 μm, the permeability decreased by 32.17%, and the strength increased from 5.83 MPa to 11.79 MPa.

Alkane organics are hydrophobic materials and the hydrophobicity is increased by the length of the carbon chain. The modification of GO by grafting alkane with different carbon chain lengths can obtain different lipophilic properties. On the other hand, alkylamines consist of hydrophobic carbon chains and hydrophilic amino groups, which can undergo amidation and substitution reactions with GO carboxylic acid sites and surface epoxy groups, respectively [121]. Hydrophobicity of modified GO can be measured by a contact angle meter. Shanmugharaj et al. [122] modified GO surface with alkylamines of different carbon chain lengths. The water contact angle measurements showed the hydrophobicity of modified GO depended on the chain length of grafted alkylamine, and the hydrophobicity was usually enhanced by the chain length. Yin et al. [123] modified GO with ODA. Then, an environmentally friendly aqueous ODA-GO modified water-based polyurethane-polyacrylate (ODA-GO/WPUA) emulsion was prepared by self-emulsification. It was found that the ODA-GO/WPUA film had high water resistance, excellent mechanical properties and good thermal stability.

Since grafting different groups can adjust the hydrophilic and lipophilic balance of GO, modified GO can be more effective as a surfactant to maintain the stability of O/W emulsion. Kumar et al. [124] conducted a functional chemical synthesis of GO using meta toluic acid and obtained functional GO through an esterification reaction. It was found that modified GO could be completely dissolved in methanol, propan-1-ol, acetone and ethanol. Zhang et al. [125] grafted PVA onto GO surface, and then successfully achieved PVA grafted GO surface by alcoholysis reaction. The prepared PVA-grafted GO/PVA nanocomposites showed excellent mechanical properties. Zhang et al. [126] modified GO with polycondensate of diethanolamine and adipic acid and used it as a surfactant to prepare MEPCM within the double-shell of PS/GO. It was found that the best stability of modified GO-stabilized emulsion was when the PH value was 2, and the encapsulation efficiency of n-hexadecane was as high as 78.5%. In addition, nano inorganic materials can also be used for GO modification. Yu et al. [111] reported the in-situ formation process of iron oxide nanoparticles on the GO surface to reduce the negative charge of graphene oxide and adjust the amphiphilicity. The modified GO was then used to stabilize glycidyl methacrylate (GMA) as an O/W emulsion.

Particles with two different structures or chemical compositions are called Janus particles [142]. The Janus GO nanosheets with different properties can be obtained by grafting hydrophobic chains on the single surface of GO. One surface is hydrophilic due to the presence of hydroxyl groups, and the other surface side is lipophilic due to the grafting of hydrophobic chains. Table 8 summarizes the applications and modified materials of Janus GO. During the Janus GO preparation, oily materials (such as paraffin, etc.) were commonly used as the masking materials to cover one of the surfaces of the GO nanosheet.

As shown in Fig. 15 (a), Wu et al. [143] added GO into the oil-water mixture to form a Pickering emulsion under acidic conditions (pH 5.2), and one side of GO nanosheets was masked by the paraffin particles. The other side of the GO nanosheets was then modified by dodecyl amine (DDA). After removing the oil phase paraffin, the Janus GO nanosheets with asymmetric chemical functionalization were fabricated. The experimental results showed that the modified GO Janus nanosheets can be effectively used as the stabilizer to fabricate an O/W Pickering solution for more than 3 weeks. Luo et al. [144] synthesized amphiphilic Janus GO nanosheets by manipulating hydrogen bonds. As shown in Fig. 15 (b), GO is immobilized on the surface of tapioca starch microspheres by hydrogen bonding. Then, the single-side surface hydrophobization of GO was conducted with alkylamine in ethanol at room temperature to fabricate amphiphilic Janus GO nanosheets. Compared with the Pickering emulsion template method, this method is without organic solvent and can easily recycle the isolated starch microspheres. The modified Janus GO nanoparticles can stabilize the oil-water emulsion for more than one week, which proves the modified Janus GO has good emulsification results.

Compared with GO, graphene has no hydroxyl and epoxy groups on the surface. Therefore, the modification of graphene can only be carried out on the CC bond [54]. Zhao et al. [28] used PSS to modify graphene and combined it with PVA, SMS and PEG-800 as the emulsifier to achieve the stability of Pickering emulsion. Dao et al. [50] used PVA to functionalize graphene to obtain water-dispersed graphene, and used the functionalized graphene to encapsulate SA in Pickering emulsion to prepare shape-stabilized PCM.

GQDs are an atomically thin and nano-wide planar carbon structure. Compared with GO sheets, GQDs also carry oxygenated groups, but have more active edge sites and are much smaller, more fragmented, and fluorescent due to bandgap opening [154]. Xi et al. [155] proved that amphiphilic GQDs are a zero-dimensional inorganic surfactant and can be used in the preparation of polystyrene hollow microspheres. Yang et al. [156] prepared amphiphilic GQDs by thermal reduction of GO, which can be used as surfactants to produce Pickering emulsion, prepare polymer particles, and block copolymer particles. The authors also synthesized amphiphilic GQDs by grafting different alkyl ligands (hexylamine and oleylamine) to the hydrophilic GQDs to generate the surfactant behaviour. He et al. [157] modified hydrophilic GQDs to prepare amphiphilic graphene quantum dots and found that GQDs exhibited interesting surface-active properties such as to stabilize water-in-cyclohexane Pickering emulsions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Summary of graphene-based materials modification methods
ce:section.ce:para: Due to the oxygen-containing groups on the surface of GO, GO shows good amphiphilicity which can be regulated by pH value and ion concentration. In addition, the existence of epoxy groups endows GO with excellent grafting properties. GO can adjust the hydrophilic and lipophilic balance by grafting different groups on the surface to achieve different emulsification effects. In particular, Janus GO sheets fabricated by a unilateral modification demonstrated much better stability for O/W emulsion than when fabricated by bilateral modification. Graphene is a two-dimensional material composed only of carbon atoms and can be used as a surfactant by grafting hydrophilic groups on the surface. However, the modification of graphene is relatively complex and the number of cases where it has been applied as a surfactant is less than that of GO. GQDs can be used as a zero-dimensional surfactant and have shown good amphiphilicity due to the oxygen-containing functional groups.

This paper reviewed preparation methods and characterisations of MEPCM with graphene-based materials. The influence of various graphene-based materials on the properties of MEPCM was studied and compared, such as thermal conductivity, phase change temperature, particle size distribution, anti-leakage property, mechanical strength, and photothermal conversion. The in-situ polymerization method is the most common method for the preparation of graphene-based MEPCM. Moreover, GO is more commonly used in the preparation of MEPCM than graphene due to its amphiphilic properties, which results in a better morphology of microcapsules.

There are many standard instruments and methods for evaluating the properties of graphene-based MEPCM capsules. Thermal conductivity meter, DSC, TGA, laser scattering particle size distribution analyser, and nanoindentation are commonly used to characterise the thermal conductivity, latent heat of phase change, thermal stability, particle size distribution, and mechanical properties of MEPCM. Characterisation results showed that when graphene was embedded in the MEPCM shell, the thermal conductivity of MEPCM was increased to as high as 7.2 W/(m∙K), which was about 7 times higher than that of adding GO into MEPCM. In addition, the additional graphene-based materials can act as a nucleating agent to reduce the subcooling of MEPCM. The addition of graphene also reduced the leakage rate of MEPCM by more than 90%, the yield stress of MEPCM increased to 1.85 MPa, and the photothermal conversion efficiency of MEPCM reached up to 90%. However, adding graphene-based material leads to a lower core-to-shell ratio and therefore a slight decrease in latent heat.

In addition, this paper also reviews the application of graphene-based material as a surfactant in the preparation of oil-water emulsions. GO is amphiphilic material and can be used in the preparation of Pickering emulsions. At the same time, the amphiphilicity of GO can be adjusted by pH value, ions, and surface modification. In particular, Janus GO is obtained by modifying one single surface of GO, which can effectively maintain the stability of oil-water emulsions. On the other hand, graphene is a two-dimensional nanomaterial consisting of only carbon atoms and exhibits strong hydrophobicity, but can also be used in the preparation of Pickering emulsions after surface modification. GQDs are a zero-dimensional surfactant due to their oxygen-containing functional groups similar to GO and exhibit good amphiphilic properties. The reviewed studies show the potential applications of graphene-based materials as surfactants for MEPCM preparation, which could also enable the addition of these materials into MEPCM at different locations by various microencapsulation methods.

Currently, most of the research studies mainly focus on the improvement of one single particular property of MEPCM. For instance, increase of thermal conductivity of MEPCM prepared with graphene is completed by using self-assembly of graphene on the surface of microcapsules, however this would result in poor morphology. The morphology of MEPCM fabricated with GO is good, but in terms of thermal conductivity and photothermal conversion, its performance is worse than graphene. Therefore, it is necessary to synthesise the advantages of different MEPCM preparation methods and procedures to develop novel high-quality MEPCM. For example, to prepare MEPCM by GO and then reduce GO to graphene by chemical reducing reaction to increase the thermal conductivity of MEPCM; to fabricate MEPCM using GO and then through self-assembly methods coat graphene on the surface of MEPCM to achieve good morphology and higher thermal conductivity. The other problem is the reduction of the latent heat of MEPCM after adding graphene-based materials. However, some research proved that the addition of graphene into PCM as a nucleating agent can increase the latent heat of PCM. In the end, the preparation of MEPCM with graphene/GO as a nucleating agent for PCM may enhance its latent heat at a certain level. In addition, the above research also proved that graphene-based materials could be integrated into MEPCM at various positions, such as PCM core, hybrids shells, and external surface of MEPCM, mix with PCM cores, wall monomers, and by self-assembly after encapsulation. By combining these methods, it is possible to control the addition location of graphene-based materials in multiple locations of MEPCM. The amphiphilic is the main property for realizing the addition location control of graphene-based materials in MEPCM. Therefore, the adjustment and modification of the amphiphilic property of graphene-based materials is a key research topic for the development of oil-water emulsion and MEPCM in future.
